name: Greek Mythology
weaknesses: Mirrors: Cannot see her own reflection without petrifying herself
Indirect Vision: Can be fought by reflecting her gaze or using periphery
Mortal Body: Despite her powers, her physical form can be wounded
Emotional Vulnerability: Attachment can cloud her judgment
abilities: Petrifying Gaze: Anyone who looks directly at her face turns instantly to stone
Serpent Hair: Living, venomous snakes that writhe and strike independently
Flight: Golden wings enable swift aerial movement
Sonic Screech (capable of causing disorientation and fear)
Rapid Reproduction (to create large, overwhelming flocks)
habitat: Solitary caves and temples, places of desolation
behavior: Victim turned protector, vengeful yet sympathetic, isolation-driven

keyMyths.title: Heracles' Sixth Labor
keyMyths.description: As his sixth labor, Heracles was tasked by King Eurystheus to drive away the Stymphalian Birds from Lake Stymphalus. He accomplished this with the help of Athena, who provided him with a bronze rattle to scare them into flight.

keyMyths.title: The Nature of the Birds
keyMyths.description: Accounts vary, but the birds are often described as having bronze beaks, sharp metallic feathers they could launch as projectiles, and poisonous excrement. Some versions even depict them as man-eating.

keyMyths.title: Habitat of the Birds
keyMyths.description: The Stymphalian Birds infested Lake Stymphalus in Arcadia, a region known for its dense forests and marshy terrain. Their numbers were so great that they blotted out the sun.

keyMyths.title: Possible Origin Myth
keyMyths.description: Some scholars suggest the Stymphalian Birds were originally nymphs transformed into birds as punishment for some transgression against the gods. This explains their unnatural characteristics.

keyMyths.title: Symbolic Interpretations
keyMyths.description: Beyond the literal interpretation, the Stymphalian Birds are often seen as representing the wild, untamed forces of nature, or the dangers lurking in the remote and uncivilized parts of the world. Heracles' defeat of them symbolizes the triumph of civilization over savagery.
keyMyths.source: Karl Kerenyi, The Heroes of the Greeks

sources.name: Apollodorus, Bibliotheca
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, providing accounts of many heroes and monsters.
sources.title: Apollodorus, Bibliotheca

sources.name: Pausanias, Description of Greece
sources.description: A travelogue of ancient Greece, offering descriptions of various locations and their associated myths.
sources.title: Pausanias, Description of Greece

sources.name: Diodorus Siculus, Library of History
sources.description: A universal history that includes mythological accounts and historical narratives.
sources.title: Diodorus Siculus, Library of History

sources.name: Robert Graves, The Greek Myths
sources.description: A modern retelling and analysis of Greek myths, drawing on various ancient sources.
sources.title: Robert Graves, The Greek Myths

sources.name: Karl Kerenyi, The Heroes of the Greeks
sources.description: A Jungian interpretation of Greek heroes and their myths.
sources.title: Karl Kerenyi, The Heroes of the Greeks

sources.name: Hesiod, Works and Days
sources.description: A didactic poem that touches upon mythological themes and the nature of the world.
sources.title: Hesiod, Works and Days
